message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="team-lead" summary="All done, shutting down team">
All work complete. Great session — the Driver-Reviewer model worked well, zero concurrent edit conflicts. The consensus-before-push pipeline is now codified. Shutting down the team. Thank you all.
</teammate-message>

input.content:
Great session. The domain architecture held up well across all five tasks -- not a single change needed to the domain layer. That's the payoff of type-driven design: a stable core that the presentation layer adapts around, not the other way around.

Quick summary of what I watched for and confirmed across all reviews:
- Domain types (`TodoItem`, `TodoTitle`, `ValidatedEmail`, etc.) unchanged
- Service contracts (`update_todo_title`, `add_todo`, etc.) reused without modification
- Authorization checks consistent across all new/modified handlers
- Error types stayed domain-specific, no infrastructure leakage
- The "parse, don't validate" boundary contract preserved (hidden input fix for `password_confirmation`)

The pipeline update (`LocalGreen -> ConsensusReached -> Pushed -> CIGreen`) was a good process improvement. Ready to shut down.
input.summary: Session summary, ready to shut down

routing.summary: Session summary, ready to shut down

Great session. The domain architecture held up well across all five tasks -- not a single change needed to the domain layer. That's the payoff of type-driven design: a stable core that the presentation layer adapts around, not the other way around.

Quick summary of what I watched for and confirmed across all reviews:
- Domain types (`TodoItem`, `TodoTitle`, `ValidatedEmail`, etc.) unchanged
- Service contracts (`update_todo_title`, `add_todo`, etc.) reused without modification
- Authorization checks consistent across all new/modified handlers
- Error types stayed domain-specific, no infrastructure leakage

content.text: Session complete. Sent my final summary to the team lead. Across all five tasks plus the pipeline update, the domain layer remained completely stable -- zero changes to domain types, services, or error handling. That's the whole point of making illegal states unrepresentable and keeping the domain pure: the core is resilient to presentation-layer evolution.
